coredata.dc:title: Mucus-inspired biomass-derived carbon dots-based solvent-free nanofluid with polyelectrolytes networks toward excellent green lubrication
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Long
Ge, Shanqin
Cai, Qingzhao
Li, Wei
Gong, Genxiang
Wu, Junhao
Wang, Huan
Yu, Jinhong
Nishimura, Kazuhito
Jiang, Nan
Cai, Tao
coredata.dc:description: Mechanical friction and wear account for 23 % of global energy, highlighting a need for eco-friendly lubricants. Drawing inspiration from marine organisms' adsorption proteins, we have developed carbon dots-based solvent-free nanofluid. Incorporating hydrophilic polyelectrolytes, the nanofluid forms three-dimensional adsorption-lubrication networks, leveraging carbon dots' strong chelating affinity, synergistically enhancing lubrication. Using the UMT-3 TriboLab and a 3D optical profilometer, we assessed the tribological performance of nanofluid and remarkably reduced the friction coefficient by 47.3 % and wear rate by 90.8 % compared to PEG 200 (Polyethylene Glycol with an average molecular weight of 200). Additionally, the infrared camera detected that nanofluid as water additives, significantly reduce wear and heat generation, decreasing temperature rise by 4.7 times. This biomimetic approach paves the way for green, oil-free, and high-performance water-based lubrication technologies.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bio-mass
Carbon dots
Solvent-free nanofluid
Polyelectrolytes
Green lubrication

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Lubricating properties of the nanofluid as water additive
The relationship between frictional heat and lubrication

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the preparation of carbon dots-based solvent-free nanofluid.

(a) Schematic diagram of the preparation of CDs@PDDA and nanofluid. (b-d) The TEM, HRTEM and average particle size images of the CDs@PDDA. (e-g) The TEM, HRTEM and average particle size images of the nanofluid. (h) FTIR spectra of CDs@PDDA and nanofluid. (i) Zeta potential of ADS, CDs@PDDA and nanofluid. (j) TG curves of CDs@PDDA, PEGPBS and nanofluid.

(a) Viscosity of Mineral oil, PAO 100 and nanofluid as a function of temperature. (b) Viscosity of Mineral oil, PAO 100 and nanofluid as a function of shear rate. (c-e) Storage modulus, loss modulus of Mineral oil, PAO 100 and nanofluid as a function of temperature.

(a) Coefficient of frictions of nanofluids with different carbon dots content (e.g., Nanofluid-3wt% refers to 3 wt% of carbon dots content in nanofluid). (b) Average coefficient of friction for samples with different carbon dots content. (c) Wear rate of Dry friction, PEG 200 and nanofluids. (d1, e1, f1) Worn tracks profile of Dry friction, PEG 200 and Nanofluid-3wt%. (d2, e2, f2) SEM images of Dry friction, PEG 200 and Nanofluid-3wt%. (d3, e3, f3) 3D profile of Dry friction, PEG 200 and Nanofluid-3wt%. (d4, e4, f4) AFM roughness map of Dry friction, PEG 200 and Nanofluid-3wt%.
Contact angle test of Water, CDs@PDDA and Water-3.

(a) Coefficient of friction of nanofluid water-based lubricant with various additives amounts (e.g., Water-3 refers to 3 wt% of Nanofluid-3wt% content in water). (b) Average coefficient of friction for all samples. (c) Wear rate of for all samples. (d1, e1, f1) Wear depth profile of Water, CDs@PDDA and Water-3. (d2, e2, f2) SEM images of worn surface morphologies of Water, CDs@PDDA and Water-3. (d3, e3, f3) 3D profile of Water, CDs@PDDA and Water-3. (d4, e4, f4) AFM roughness map of Water, CDs@PDDA and Water-3.

Tribothermal testing of water and Water-3. (a) Coefficient of friction curve of Water and Water-3. (b) Absolute temperature profiles for water and Water-3. (c) Thermography of Water and Water-3 over different time periods.

(a) Schematic diagram of the inside and outside of the steel plate worn track of Nanofluid-3wt%. (b) TEM images of the nanofluid outside the worn track. (c) The average particle size of the carbon dots outside the worn track. (d) TEM images of the nanofluid inside the worn track. (e-f) The enlarged area in (d) and the average particle size of the carbon dots in the nanofluid of inside the worn track.

Characterization of the friction film on the surface of the steel substrate under lubrication of Nanofluid-3wt% after 30 min of friction test. HRTEM image of cross-sectional friction film and EDS element maps (supplemented with FIB).
XPS spectra of worn surface with Nanofluid-3wt% lubrication.
Schematic diagram of the wear mechanism of carbon dot-based solvent-free nanofluid lubrication.
Names of all nanofluids and Nanofluid-3wt% as water-based lubricants.

row.entry: The concentration of CDs@PDDA in nanofluid
The concentration of Nanofluid−3wt% in water

head.ce:title: Mucus-inspired biomass-derived carbon dots-based solvent-free nanofluid with polyelectrolytes networks toward excellent green lubrication

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Northeast Petroleum University, Daqing 163318, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Northeast Petroleum University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Northeast Petroleum University, Daqing, 163318, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Advanced Marine Materials, Ningbo Institute of Materials Technology and Engineering, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Ningbo 315201, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Advanced Marine Materials, Ningbo Institute of Materials Technology and Engineering, Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Advanced Nano-processing Engineering Lab, Mechanical Engineering, Kogakuin University, Tokyo 192-0015, Japan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Advanced Nano-processing Engineering Lab, Mechanical Engineering, Kogakuin University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Advanced Nano-processing Engineering Lab, Mechanical Engineering, Kogakuin University, Tokyo 192–0015, Japan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Mechanical friction and wear account for 23 % of global energy, highlighting a need for eco-friendly lubricants. Drawing inspiration from marine organisms' adsorption proteins, we have developed carbon dots-based solvent-free nanofluid. Incorporating hydrophilic polyelectrolytes, the nanofluid forms three-dimensional adsorption-lubrication networks, leveraging carbon dots' strong chelating affinity, synergistically enhancing lubrication. Using the UMT-3 TriboLab and a 3D optical profilometer, we assessed the tribological performance of nanofluid and remarkably reduced the friction coefficient by 47.3 % and wear rate by 90.8 % compared to PEG 200 (Polyethylene Glycol with an average molecular weight of 200). Additionally, the infrared camera detected that nanofluid as water additives, significantly reduce wear and heat generation, decreasing temperature rise by 4.7 times. This biomimetic approach paves the way for green, oil-free, and high-performance water-based lubrication technologies.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The novel full green lubrication concept based on carbon dots and biomimicry in this work.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors are unable or have chosen not to specify which data has been used.

ce:section.ce:para: Given that mechanical friction and wear consume 23 % of the world's energy, and that this significant energy loss is linked to greenhouse gas emissions and environmental degradation, the development of environmentally friendly lubrication technologies is increasingly urgent [1,2]. Lubricants are typically composed of base oil and additives, and some greases also incorporate thickeners. Nano additives have been proven to have excellent tribological properties, including anti-wear and friction-reduction, across a range of base oil and greases [3,4]. However, on one hand, the weak film-forming ability on the friction surface and poor dispersibility of nano additives in base oil have been key issues hindering their further application in the field of tribology [5,6]. On the other hand, the base oil often faces the problems of being difficult to degrade, causing serious environmental burden. Among them, the traditional solvent refining mineral oil has limited performance and great impact on the environment. Some synthetic oils have excellent thermal stability and oxidation resistance, regular molecular structure, and high environmental friendliness, but the production cost is high [7,8]. Therefore, it is particularly important to explore new green lubrication systems to balance the growing demand for high performance lubricants with the increasing environmental burden.

Carbon dots, as an emerging zero-dimensional nanomaterial, offer advantages such as low toxicity [9], environmental friendliness [10], biocompatibility [11], and ease of preparation [12], and are widely used in the fields of medicine [13], optical devices [14], and composite materials [15]. Carbon dots' exceptional thermal and chemical stability render them an affordable nanomaterial increasingly utilized in the field of lubrication. Mou et al. reacted carbon dots with ionic liquids to obtain ionically grafted carbon dots, which showed good dispersibility and lubricity, and formed a lubricating film through rolling and electrostatic adsorption [16]. Hu et al. synthesized environmentally friendly carbon dots using a one-pot method. The carbon dots, with highly electronegative N and O elements on their surface, form a monolayer film on the metal surface by electrostatic adsorption [17]. The surface functionalization of carbon dots not only enhances their adsorption on the surface of friction pair, thereby achieving a rolling ball and lubricating film effects, but also their environmentally friendly nature contributes to a minimal environmental burden. Considering the aforementioned characteristics, it is very promising to construct a new green lubrication system centered around carbon dots.

In nature, marine organisms such as mussels can tightly adhere to foreign surface in sea water. This is because mussels can secrete an adhesive protein (mucus) that contains various residues, such as DOPA (3,4-dihydroxyphenylalanine), tyrosine, phenylalanine, as well as cations, anions, and uncharged groups. These protein chains not just have a strong chelating effect on metal ions, but due to the free combination of these protein residues, can self-assemble into a network structure [18,19]. This structure endows it with super adhesiveness to rocks and allows it to firmly adhere to the surface. These lubricating proteins with multiple residues can reduce the friction between mussels and the rock surface as they move [20]. Given these unique characteristics, constructing a biomimetic mucus system based on carbon dots as a new type of lubricating material, with multiple adsorption sites that can help carbon dots firmly adhere to the surfaces of friction pairs and work together to exert lubricating effects, seems to be a very promising approach.

PDDA (Polydiallyl dimethylammonium chloride), as an environmentally friendly, biodegradable and water-soluble strong electrolyte, exhibits excellent dispersibility in water and has a rich presence of positively charged N+ on its surface [21,22]. Depending on its concentration in aqueous solutions, PDDA displays different polyelectrolytes morphologies. At high concentrations, PDDA forms spherical micelle structures, while at low concentrations, it presents ordinary long-chain structure [23]. The abundant N+ in PDDA provides sufficient adsorption sites for the contact surface, and its single-chain structure can easily form a networked adsorption polyelectrolytes structure [24]. Combining it as a carbon dots surface modifier with another polyelectrolyte that contains groups with opposite charges can produce a carbon dots-based nanofluid with fluid properties and multiple adsorption effects, thereby generating an adhesive and self-lubricating effect similar to that of mucus.

In this work, carbon dots-based solvent-free nanofluid with multi-coordination and multi-spatial arrangement structures had been developed. Adenine (ADS), a biomass derived from shrimps, soybeans and fishes, was chosen to combine low-concentration PDDA to prepare PDDA-modified carbon dots (CDs@PDDA) with a high yield of 20 % using fast yet low energy-consuming microwave methods. Then CDs@PDDA was combined with polyethylene glycol 4-nonylphenyl 3-sulfopropyl ether (PEGPBS) to prepare carbon dots-based solvent-free nanofluid by proton exchange reaction. PEGPBS has good biocompatibility and degradability, it is an environmentally friendly ionotropic polymer. Therefore the characteristics of such as-prepared nanofluid can be summarized into four main points: (i) precursor materials derived from biomass and polyelectrolytes that construct three-dimensional structures are environmentally friendly polymer materials; (ii) the carbon dots prepared from Adenine have a strong chelating effect on metal ions [25], which allows for the rapid and uniform distribution of carbon dots on the surface of friction pairs; (iii) the positive charges of PDDA provide electrostatic adsorption on the steel plate surface and offer reaction sites for the subsequently introduced PEGPBS; (iv) due to the characteristics of the two polyelectrolytes, the as-prepared nanofluid has good dispersibility in water. The lubricating performance of the carbon dots-based solvent-free nanofluid was investigated by varying the ratio and concentration of CDs@PDDA and PEGPBS. The results showed that whether as an inherent lubricant or as an additives for green water-based lubrication, the as-prepared nanofluid exhibited excellent lubricating properties. Surface analysis techniques were used to systematically analyze the potential lubrication mechanisms. Additionally, real-time recording and analysis of the relationship between frictional heat and coefficient of friction were conducted.

Adenine (99 %), Ethanol (99.7 %), Polyethylene glycol (99.8 %), PDDA (20 % w/v; MW 100,000, 99 %) purchased from Aladdin Chemical Co., Ltd. (China). Polyethylene glycol 4-nonyl 3-sulfopropyl ether (PEGPBS) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Louis, MO, USA). All chemicals are used as received, without further purification. Deionized water was used for all experiments.

Using Adenine and PDDA solution as raw materials, CDs@PDDA was prepared using a microwave method [26]. Firstly, 1 g of Adenine was placed in 500 ml of hot deionized water and stirred until the Adenine powder was completely dissolved. Secondly, 0.416 g of PDDA solution was added and dissolved in the prepared water solution. The solution was microwaved at 900 W for 6 min to produce a carbon dots solution, then cooled to room temperature. Subsequently, the solution was filtered using a 0.22 µm filter membrane and dialyzed for 24 h using a 3500 kDa dialysis bag to remove excess residues, resulting in the final CDs@PDDA water solution. Additionally, the CDs@PDDA water solution was freeze-dried for 48 h to obtain 0.216 g of white powder, and we calculated the specific value of the final carbon dots mass and the precursor mass to achieve a yield of 20 % approximately. In addition, by controlling the molecular weight of adenine and PDDA, we prepared three other types of carbon dots (Fig. S1).

Firstly, 25 mg of CDs@PDDA powder was dispersed in 10 ml of deionized water. Then, 0.5 g of Polyethylene glycol 4-nonylphenyl 3-sulfopropyl ether (PEGPBS) was dissolved in 20 ml of deionized water. The prepared CDs@PDDA water solution was slowly added to the PEGPBS water solution in batches while stirring continuously until the reaction is completed. Dialyzed the resulting solution using a 3500 kDa dialysis bags for 12 h to remove residues. Collected the dialyzed carbon dots-based solvent-free nanofluid in a beaker and dried them at 50 °C in a vacuum oven for 4 h to obtain light yellow carbon-dots based solvent-free nanofluid ( Fig. 1). As shown in Table 1, we defined different proportions of carbon-dots based solvent-free nanofluid according to the mass ratios of CDs@PDDA (0.5 wt%, 1 wt%, 3 wt% and 5 wt%) in the nanofluid as Nanofluid-0.5 wt%, Nanofluid-1wt%, Nanofluid-3wt% and Nanofluid-5wt%. At the same time, we defined the samples when Nanofluid-3wt% is used as water additive.

The morphology and particle size distribution were characterized using a transmission electron microscope (TEM, Talos F200X from Thermo Scientific). The chemical structure of the carbon dots-based solvent-free nanofluid was analyzed using a Fourier-transform infrared spectrometer (FT-IR, model IS 50 from the USA) and a thermogravimetric analyzer (TGA, model STA449F3 from Germany). Zeta potential distribution data were obtained using the laser particle size analyzer Nano-ZS (Malvern, UK), with ethanol as the solvent. The rheological behavior was tracked using a rotational shear rheometer (TA, Instruments ARG2), with an angular frequency of 10 rad/s, temperature range of 25–70 °C, and shear rate range of 0–80 s−1. Coefficient of frictions of all samples were tested under reciprocating sliding mode at room temperature using the (UMT-3 TriboLab, USA), the steel plate size is 20 mm × 20 mm× 3 mm, and the diameter of the steel ball is 6 mm. The applied load, stroke length, velocity, and test time were set to 5 N, 5 mm, 5 mm/s, and 1800 s, respectively. Wear volume and worn area were measured using a non-contact optical profilometer (Rtec Up-Lambda, California, USA). The scanning electron microscope (SEM, Zeiss G300 from Germany) equipped with energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) was used to analyze the worn tracks and element distribution. Thermal imaging video of the friction process was taken with an infrared camera (Fluke Ti400, USA). A four-ball friction machine (MS-10 A, Tenkey, China) was used to take the friction test under the condition of water additives lubrication of Nanofluid-3wt%, and the test conditions were 150 N, 500 r/min and 540 s. The element distribution analysis of the lubrication film on the cross-section of the worn tracks was conducted using focused ion beam (FIB, Auriga from Germany) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM, Talos F200X from the USA). Furthermore, the chemical composition and electronic binding energy of the worn surface were characterized using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, model Axis Ultra DLD from the UK) to further elucidate the lubrication mechanism.

As a zero-dimensional nanoparticle, carbon dots have unique tribological properties such as rolling effect and repairing effect [27]. Constructing a three-dimensional structure on the surface of carbon dots is beneficial for enhancing their adsorption efficiency on the surface, thereby achieving superior lubricating effects. Fig. 2a shows a flowchart for the preparation of nanofluid. Fig. 2b is the TEM image of CDs@PDDA, Fig. 2c and Fig. 2d shows the HRTEM image and average particle size map of the CDs@PDDA, respectively. It can be seen that the lattice spacing of the CDs@PDDA is 0.21 nm, and the diameter of the carbon dots particles is about 2.41 ± 1.2 nm, indicating the presence of a graphite-like structure. Fig. 2e shows the TEM image of nanofluid. Fig. 2f and Fig. 2g show the HRTEM image and average particle size map of the nanofluid, respectively. The lattice spacing of carbon dots in nanofluid is 0.21 nm, and the diameter of carbon dots is about 2.37 ± 0.7 nm. After grafting PEGPBS, the polymer content increased, but size of the carbon dots did not change significantly. Infrared spectroscopy was employed to characterize the CDs@PDDA and nanofluid (Fig. 2h). The peaks at 1680 cm−1 and 1600 cm−1 are attributed to the vibrations of CN and CC in the Adenine structure, respectively, and the peaks at 1350 cm−1 and 1460 cm−1 are attributed to C-N and -CH3 in PDDA, respectively. At the same time, multiple R-SO2-O- peaks appeared in the interval of 950 cm−1 and 1190 cm−1, indicating that the PEGPBS had been modified to the surface of the CDs@PDDA to form nanofluid. In order to explore the possibility of nanofluid as water-based additives, we used a laser particle size analyzer to test the zeta potential of aqueous solutions dispersed with a certain concentration of ADS, CDs@PDDA and nanofluid. As can be seen from Fig. 2i, ADS has two peaks of opposite potentials after being dispersed in water, indicating the dispersion is unstable. On the contrary CDs@PDDA shows a positive potential of + 42.5 mV while the nanofluid has a potential peak of −32 mV, indicating they both have excellent dispersion in water. In order to further verify the degree of grafting of the nanofluid, thermogravimetric analysis (TG) was performed on the CDs@PDDA, PEGPBS and Nanofluid in the range of RT-800 °C, and the temperature-dependent trend of CDs@PDDA, PEGPBS, and Nanofluid are shown in Fig. 2j, and the weight loss of the nanofluid in the first stage is attributed to the PEGPBS decomposition at 180 °C, followed by the decomposition of PDDA at 270 °C. Compared to PDDA directly linked to carbon dots, the outer layer of PEGPBS decomposes firstly.

Nanofluid has gained significant prospects in the field of green lubrication as solid-liquid hybrid lubricants due to their excellent liquid flow behavior without solvents [28]. We tested the viscosity and modulus of Mineral oil and PAO 100 using a rotational shear rheometer and compared the results with nanofluid. In general, the increase in temperature leaded to increased thermal motion of molecules, increased molecular spacing, and reduced inter-molecular attraction, which made it easier for liquids to flow and thus reduced viscosity. In Fig. 3a and b, according to the temperature viscosity properties, the viscosity of Mineral oil, PAO 100 and nanofluid decreased as the varies with temperature and shear rate [29]. The viscosity of nanofluid was greater than that of Mineral oil and PAO 100, which was beneficial for the formation of a lubricating film on the surface of the friction pair.

The G'' (loss modulus) of Mineral oil and PAO 100 were almost higher than the G' (storage modulus), indicated that the lubricant retains its liquid fluidity as the temperature increased (Fig. 3c and d). This result showed that Mineral oil and PAO 100 exhibited mainly viscous behavior at different temperatures, and at the same time were thermally stable, which was suitable for friction applications at different temperature conditions. Fig. 3e indicated the change of G'' and G' of nanofluid with temperature, the G′' was higher than the G′ at the initial temperature, and nanofluid exhibited good liquidity. As temperature raised, interactions within the nanofluid intensified, promoted the formation of a stable, physical cross-linked structure that raised the G′. When the G′ of the nanofluid was higher than G′', the lubricating film exhibited strong elastic properties, which helped to maintain the stability of the film and resist external shear deformation, thereby provided better lubrication effect. Unlike Mineral oil and PAO 100, by controlling the temperature of the nanofluid, it exhibits different viscoelastic properties and can be applied to different mechanical lubrication.

The main function of lubricant is to reduce friction and wear between friction pairs. Therefore, in order to verify the concept of oil-free lubrication, we evaluated the tribological properties of nanofluid through tribological experiments. After characterizing the basic structure and rheological properties of nanofluids, we prepared four types of nanofluids by varying the ratio of carbon dots to PEGPBS and analysis their tribological behavior. In Fig. 4a, the average coefficient of friction of Dry friction was about 0.56, and the coefficient of friction changed drastically in the first 600 s period, reaching 0.66 at the highest time, and then the coefficient of friction tended to be flat. But it was always higher than 0.5, this was the result of the absence of lubricant between the steel ball and the steel plate. This lack of lubricating medium leaded to the direct contact of the friction pairs, which greatly increased the wear rate of the steel plate, reaching 4.6 × 10−4 mm3/N·m. Compared to Dry friction, PEG 200 lubricates significantly better, which was due to the physicochemical action on the friction surface. The average coefficient of friction of PEG 200 was about 0.15, the wear rate was 1.01 × 10−5 mm3/N·m. The coefficient of friction curve fluctuates all the time, this was because PEG 200 cannot be well adsorbed on the surface of the steel plate. Different from the above two lubrication results, the coefficient of friction of all nanofluids were generally below 0.1, and the friction curves were very stable, with almost no fluctuations in the friction process. The mass proportion of CDs@PDDA in nanofluids was increased from 0.5 wt% to 5 wt%, and we observed a trend of decreasing coefficient of friction and wear rate followed by an increase with increasing carbon dots concentration (Fig. 4b and c). When the addition concentration of CDs@PDDA was 0.5 wt% and 1 wt%, due to the insufficient carbon dots content, an effective lubricating film could not be formed in the initial stage of friction, resulting in an increase in coefficient of friction and wear rate. And at a CDs@PDDA content of 5 wt%, carbon dots tended to accumulate in large quantities on the surface of the friction pairs and caused the continuity of the lubricating film to be disrupted. When the content of the CDs@PDDA in the nanofluid was 3 wt%, the lowest coefficient of friction and wear rate were obtained simultaneously, which are 0.079 and 9.3 × 10−7 mm3/N·m, respectively, and the running-in period was less than 3 s. Compared with Dry friction and PEG 200, the coefficient of friction was reduced by 85.8 % and 47.3 %, respectively, and the wear rate was reduced by 99.8 % and 90.8 %, respectively. This optimal concentration of carbon dots in the nanofluid which could make carbon dots stably arranged in the three-dimensional adsorption lubrication structure formed by the polyelectrolytes, quickly form a lubricating film at the beginning of friction, and firmly adsorb on the surface of the steel plate during the friction process. This adsorption was mainly attributed to the film-forming effect of PDDA and the self-chelation of carbon dots. The polyethylene glycol chain contained in the PEGPBS also played a lubricating role. Although some carbon dots were insufficient or accumulated, they could still exert the chelating effect of the carbon dots themselves on the rigid surface, and cooperate with the adsorption effect of the nanofluid themselves, resulting in the coefficient of friction and wear rate still had obvious advantages over Dry friction and PEG 200 lubrication.

To investigate the surface conditions of the steel plates during the friction process, we characterized the worn tracks. From left to right, the images showed the profile, scanning, 3D profile, and roughness of the wear tracks (Fig. 4d, e, and f). The cross-sections of the Dry friction and PEG 200 were deep and rough while the worn depth of the Nanofluid-3wt% was small. The worn profile of Nanofluid-3wt% (Fig. 4f1) exhibited minimal fluctuations compared to Dry friction (Fig. 4d1) and PEG 200 (Fig. 4e1). Under scanning electron microscopy (Fig. 4f2), it was observed that the width of the wear track was approximately 146 µm, and the worn surface appeared smooth. In contrast, both Dry friction and PEG 200 showed wider wear tracks (Fig. 4d2 and e2). This was due to the fact that Dry friction results in a large amount of abrasive worn, with extremely rough worn surface and rough and deep grooves. PEG 200 did not form an effective protective film, and the friction pairs collided with each other, causing hard particles to scratch the worn surface. Compared to Dry friction and PEG 200, Nanofluid-3wt% has a smoother surface. This difference was also evident in the 3D profile images, where the maximum depth of the worn track of Nanofluid-3wt% was approximately 0.64 µm, compared with Dry friction and PEG 200, both the width and depth were much lower (Fig. 4d3, e3 and f3). To indicate the change in roughness of the worn surface, we performed a roughness measurement of the abrasion surface with AFM. In roughness maps, where roughness of the worn track of Nanofluid-3wt% was approximately 3.47 nm, narrower than those of Dry friction and PEG 200 (Fig. 4d4, e4 and f4). The results showed that Nanofluid-3wt% rubbed steel plate has a smoother surface of worn track and exhibited the best anti-wear effect.

Furthermore, we compared the friction performance of the other three nanofluids with different contents of carbon dots (Fig. S2). Nanofluid-0.5 wt% and Nanofluid-1wt% exhibited good friction performance, with lower depths and widths of worn tracks as well.

as lower surface roughness compared to Dry friction and PEG 200. Nanofluid-5wt% showed larger fluctuations in worn track, likely due to the increased content of carbon dots leading to the accumulation of carbon dots during the friction process, resulting in decreased friction performance.

We performed scanning analysis on the steel ball surface. The steel ball surface lubricated with Nanofluid-3wt% exhibited only shallow grooves, and elemental analysis of the worn tracks area did not reveal significant accumulation of carbon oxides (Fig. S3). The worn tracks appeared smooth and intact on steel plate, with no apparent grooves when magnified. The distribution of elements was uniform, with N and S elements originating from PDDA and PEGPBS, respectively. The results showed that PEGPBS can be well grafted to the surface of the carbon dots, and this lubrication structure is constructed in synergy with PDDA, which effectively reduces the wear of the steel ball surface (Fig. S4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Lubricating properties of the nanofluid as water additive
ce:section.ce:para: Water is a clean resource with a lubricating effect, exhibits different lubricating properties on the surface of different materials. However, when used as a lubricant for mechanical lubrication of steel, water exhibits poor lubrication effect. The addition of additives to water can often greatly improve its friction properties, but it also comes with environmental pollution caused by additives or the lack of water-based additives. In order to explore the high performance eco-friendly additives for green water-based lubricating, we added nanofluid to water to evaluate their lubricating properties as water-based additives. Carbon dots-based solvent-free nanofluid consists of nanoparticles and polyelectrolytes. Combining the results with the zeta potential tests, the three-dimensional adsorption-lubrication networks of carbon dots-based solvent-free nanofluid demonstrates excellent dispersibility and adsorption properties, which shows good lubrication potential when used as an additive in the water. As shown in Fig. 5, it can be observed that the contact angle of water on the steel plate surface is 56.57°. The CDs@PDDA, which is positively charged, can electrostatically adsorb onto the surface of the steel plate. This results in a strong interaction between the CDs@PDDA and the steel plate. This force may cause an increase in the spacing between individual carbon dots, leading to an increased contact angle of 69.42°. However, the introduction of sulfonic acid groups in the structure partially neutralizes the charges and introduces polyelectrolytes chains, thus reducing the intermolecular attractive forces compared to the interaction between water molecules and the solid surface. As a consequence, the contact angle decreases to 27.14°, facilitating the adhesion of the carbon dots-based solvent-free nanofluid to the surface of the steel plate.

Following, the tribological performance of the carbon dots-based solvent-free nanofluid as water additives were investigated. To verify the effect of contact angle changes on friction performance, we introduced CDs@PDDA as an additive into water. We then compared its friction performance with that of carbon dots-based solvent-free nanofluid, which were also used as additives in water. Four water-based lubricants with different concentrations were prepared based on the mass fraction of Nanofluid-3wt% in water. The proportion of Nanofluid-3wt% in water was increased from 0.5 wt% to 5 wt%, which abbreviated as Water-0.5, Water-1, Water-3 and Water-5, respectively ( Fig. 6a). The average coefficient of friction of water was 0.47, and the average coefficient of friction curve has been in a rising state in the first 900 s, and the curve fluctuated greatly. It was worth noting that the coefficient of friction curve of the CDs@PDDA also fluctuated greatly, with an average coefficient of friction of 0.38. This phenomenon may stem from the increased contact angle of water on the friction surface in the presence of CDs@PDDA, thus making the formation of the lubricating film more difficult. CDs@PDDA exhibited greater wear than water, reached 9.8 × 10−6 mm3/N·m. During the friction process, the surface of the carbon dots was grafted with PDDA, which has multiple charge centers and features an ultra-long carbon chain structure that was relatively rigid. While this characteristic allows for multi-point adsorption, the rigidity of these molecular chains can cause entanglement, potentially resulting in the encapsulation of the core carbon dots and hindering their ability to effectively perform their lubricating performance. Compared to the lubrication effect of water and CDs@PDDA, nanofluid exhibited excellent performance as water additives. In particular, the lowest and relatively stable coefficient of friction and wear rate can be obtained at an optimal concentration of 3 wt%. The coefficient of friction and wear rate of Water-3 were approximately 0.16 and 2.3 × 10−7 mm3/N·m, respectively. Compared to water, the coefficient of friction and wear rate of Water-3 decreased by 63 % and 53 %, respectively (Fig. 6b and c). This was the result of the collaborative action between carbon dots and the outer layer of polyelectrolytes, specifically polyethylene glycol, characterized by its flexible structure, within nanofluid. The good water solubility of the polyelectrolytes on the surface of carbon dots and the chelation of carbon dots on the substrate form a layer of flexible contact between the friction pairs and reducing friction and wear significantly. Furthermore, we compared the friction performance of three other water-based lubricants and found that Water-0.5, Water-1, and Water-5 exhibited good lubricating properties, reducing wear by 48 %, 30 %, and 36 %, respectively, compared to water alone (Fig. S5).

Then, we compared the worn depth profiles, worn surface morphologies, 3D profiles, and roughness maps of Water, Water-3 and CDs@PDDA that dissolved in water (Fig. 6d, e, and f). Water-3 has the smallest wear depth fluctuation, while Water and CDs@PDDA had significant wear depth fluctuations. (Fig. 6d1, e1, and f1). In the scanning images of worn surface morphologies, the worn width of Water-3 was greater than that of Water and CDs@PDDA, probably as a result of the carbon dots spreading well on the abrasion surface (Fig. 6d2, e2, and f2). In 3D profiles images, both Water and CDs@PDDA showed deep grooves, while the maximum depth for Water-3 was only 0.84 µm (Fig. 6d3, e3, and f3). The roughness analysis of the worn tracks surface used AFM revealed that the roughness of Water-3 was 32.3 nm, which was lower than that of water friction and CDs@PDDA (Fig. 6d4, e4, and f4). In order to study the lubrication state of Water-3 on the surface of the steel plate, we performed scanning analysis on the steel ball surface and the worn tracks on the steel plate (Fig. S6 and S7). The surface of the steel ball showed a certain enrichment of carbon and oxygen elements, this may be due to oxidation of steel substrates during friction. The uniform dispersion of S and N elements across the steel plate's surface implies that the nanofluid in water can readily identify the metal substrate and promptly adsorb onto it during friction, even as an additive, it notably optimizes both friction and wear.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The relationship between frictional heat and lubrication
ce:section.ce:para: Frictional heat is generated during the friction process. As the friction process progresses, the intermolecular interactions between the surface of objects are disturbed, resulting in molecular vibration and the generation of frictional heat. This generation of huge frictional heat can lead to worn of moving parts and a reduction in the lifespan of mechanical components [31]. At the same time, it also causes energy consumption. Therefore, reducing the generation of frictional heat and reducing energy dissipation are the problems that need to be solved by the new generation of lubricants. Water is widely used as a lubricant in daily life, but its effectiveness as a mechanical lubricant is often not ideal. Under frictional conditions, as the duration of friction increases, the temperature of the water will rise rapidly. Additionally, the steam generated can lead to the corrosion of moving parts. Therefore, it is necessary to reduce frictional heat and energy dissipation.

Here, we utilized carbon dots derived from biomass in conjunction with degradable and environmentally friendly polyelectrolytes to obtain Nanofluid-3wt% as additives for water-based lubrication. Through four-balls friction tester and infrared camera, we real-time recorded the changed of coefficient of friction and temperature of water and Water-3 under the same experimental conditions of 150 N and 500 r/min. In Fig. 7a, the coefficient of friction for water was highly unstable, with fluctuations ranging from 0.15 to 0.55. In contrast, the aqueous solution containing 3 wt% nanofluid (Water-3) exhibited a stable coefficient of friction curve, which slightly increased within the range of 0.1 to 0.15, and remains relatively stable overall. The average coefficient of friction for Water-3 was significantly lower than that of water. Fig. 7b reflected the change of temperature the oil cup during the friction process. In the friction process, the temperature of Water-3 raised with a low trend and does not exceed 30 °C. In contrast, the temperature of water raised quickly, reaching its maximum value at 300 s, and then, as the lubricating film ruptures and the contact pressure decreases, the temperature change tends to be gentle. The sharp drop in the coefficient of friction curve at 300 s also confirmed this result. During the experiment, an infrared camera was used to record the temperature changes outside the oil cup in real-time (Fig. 7c). As the experiment progressed, compared to the initial temperature of the oil cup, the absolute temperature change before and after the Water-3 experiment was 1.7 °C, while the temperature change for water was 8.1 °C, which was 4.7 times higher that of Water-3. A high coefficient of friction leads to the substantial generation of frictional heat, causing the water temperature to rise quickly. In summary, Water-3 has a better lubricating effect than water, and as a green and environmentally friendly aqueous lubricant, it offers significant performance advantages in reducing friction and wear and minimizing frictional heat.

To study the mechanism behind the formation of stable and continuous lubricating film by nanofluid during the friction process [30], we conducted Raman tests on the worn areas of both the steel ball and plate (Fig. S8 and S9). The Raman peaks on the surface of the steel sphere show prominent D and G peaks at ∼1384 cm−1 and ∼1550 cm−1. The Raman spectrum on the surface of the steel plate shows Raman peaks at 1360 cm−1 and 1590 cm−1, but the D peak of the steel ball is larger than that of the steel plate. In any case, carbon dots are present on the surface of the friction pairs, which is attributed to the interaction between the nanofluid and the steel substrate. On the one hand, the carbon dots are indeed involved in the construction of this three-dimensional adsorption lubrication structure. on the other hand, the carbon dots are adsorbed on a rigid surface during friction and maintain their structure rather than exist in a non-structural form.

Furthermore, we studied the state of carbon dots-based nanofluid during lubrication and used TEM to characterize the nanofluid inside and outside the worn tracks ( Fig. 8). Fig. 8a is a schematic diagram illustrating the collection of carbon dots from both the friction area and the non-friction area after the friction process. Fig. 8b shows the TEM images of carbon dots-based nanofluid outside the worn track, with an average particle size of about 2.54 nm, which is almost the same as the particle size of the nanofluid samples before friction (Fig. 8c). Fig. 8d shows a TEM image of the carbon dots-based nanofluid in the worn track, in which a distinct spatial distribution structure emerges, which is a direct demonstration of the formation of a three-dimensional adsorption lubrication structure by the polyelectrolytes and carbon dots. We select a region in Fig. 8d for a closer examination and observed that while the carbon dots remained, their average size is slightly reduced, measured at approximately 2.09 nm (Fig. 8e and f). At the same time, TEM images revealed that the number of carbon dots within the worn track is somewhat lower than that outside the track, which may be due to their transfer to the surfaces of the friction pairs. There are carbon dots inside and outside the worn track, and the existence of three-dimensional adsorption structure provides a good environment for the formation of lubricating film. Combined with the Raman results of steel ball and steel plate surface, it can be demonstrated that carbon dots are involved in the process of forming a unique lubricating film. In the three-dimensional adsorption lubrication structure constructed by carbon dots and polyelectrolytes, the carbon dots are arranged in a certain spatial position, and the carbon dots are adsorbed on the rigid surface through their chelation effect during the friction process.

To gain a deeper understanding of the lubrication behavior, high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) and energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) imaging were employed, along with focused ion beam (FIB) assistance, to investigate the presence and distribution of various elements such as C, N, O, and Fe ( Fig. 9). It is evident that the surface of the steel plate after friction with Nanofluid-3wt% primarily contains C, N, O, and Fe elements under HRTEM. In the HRTEM images, a distinct lubricating film with a thickness of 30 nm∼50 nm can be observed at the worn section, mainly composed of carbon compounds with some impurities. At higher magnifications, clear lattice fringes are visible, which, after EDS analysis, were determined to be iron oxide compounds. HRTEM analysis reveals that carbon in the lubricating film predominantly exists in an amorphous state, which contradicts the previous Raman testing on the steel ball's surface. It is possible that the carbon deposition of the polyelectrolytes chain during friction, and the crystal structure of the carbon dots was destroyed on the worn surface during the high-energy ion beam cutting process led to it.

To investigate the lubrication mechanism of nanofluid, XPS analysis was conducted on the C 1 s, N 1 s, O 1 s and Fe 2p spectra of Nanofluid-3wt% at the worn surface. As shown in Fig. 10a, the full spectrum of elements is presented, while Fig. 10b shows the C 1 s spectrum. Under the conditions of Nanofluid-3wt% as the lubricant, the C 1 s peaks at 287.9 eV, 286.3 eV, 284.8 eV, and 282.5 eV on the worn surface are attributed to CO/CN, C-O/C-N, C-C, and CO32-, respectively. The existence of CO and CN might stem from oxidation during the friction process, while C-N and C-C originate from ADS, PDDA, and PEGPBS. The presence of CO32- may be attributed to the formation of Fe2CO3 during the friction process [32]. Fig. 10c shows the O 1 s spectrum, with peaks observed at 533.3 eV, 531.6 eV, and 530.1 eV, corresponding to C-O, CO, and Fe-O, respectively. These peaks mainly originate from the oxidation of C and O contained in the polyelectrolytes chains during the friction process [33]. N element mainly comes from the precursor ADS and PDDA, with peaks appearing at 398.7 eV and 400 eV, respectively (Fig. 10d) [34]. As for Fe, the main component of the friction pair, peaks are observed at 726.6 eV, 724.1 eV, 720.1 eV, 713.5 eV, 711 eV, and 707 eV, primarily attributed to Fe-O. The presence of Fe-O is attributed to the oxidation of Fe during friction, which leads to the appearance of Fe-O peaks (Fig. 10e) [35].

Combining the results from Raman, TEM, FIB and XPS testing, it can be determined that nanofluid form a lubricating film during the friction process. Carbon dots in nanofluid play an important role in the friction process as lubricants. It was proved by FIB and TEM that these carbon dots form a stereoscopic adsorption lubrication structure with the help of polyelectrolytes ( Fig. 11). At the onset of friction, carbon dots rapidly adsorb onto the surface of the friction pair through chelation and the electrostatic adsorption effect of PDDA on their surface, creating multiple adsorption sites. It firmly adheres to the surface of the friction pairs and works in conjunction with the self-lubricating properties of PEGPBS. At the same time, the carbon dots are able to reduce direct wear between the friction pairs through their inherent rolling, repair, and polishing actions. When it acts as an additives for water-based lubrication, carbon dots work through two mechanisms. Firstly, the adsorption of carbon dots with the friction surface. Secondly, due to the good hydrophilicity of the polyelectrolytes, when used as an additives, its three-dimensional spatial structure is looser than that when used as intrinsic lubrication, the carbon dots wrapped by the polyelectrolytes chain can continuously supplement the surface of the friction pairs to ensure the direct adsorption of carbon dots and friction pairs. This is also the reason why the frictional heat is greatly reduced.

This study proposes a novel green lubrication concept based on carbon dots and biomimicry that includes the selection of eco-friendly raw materials, energy-efficient preparation methods, environmentally friendly lubricants, and efficient lubrication strategies, which offers innovative perspectives for the development of oil-free and high-performance water-based lubricants. Tribological testing of nanofluid demonstrate that the friction coefficient of nanofluid is only 0.079, and the wear rate is 9.3 × 10−7 mm3/ N·m, which is 85.8 % and 47.3 % lower than that of Dry friction and PEG 200, respectively. When used as water additives, the coefficient of friction is only 0.16, and the wear rate is 2.3 × 10−7 mm3/N·m, which reduces the coefficient of friction and wear rate by 63 % and 53 %, respectively, compared with pure water alone. Besides, the nanofluids can significantly reduce the generation of frictional heat of water when used as a water additive, thereby achieving an effect that lowers the temperature increase by 4.7 times. The results indicate that the as-prepared nanofluids not only achieve an excellent oil-free lubricating effect but also enhance the tribological properties of green water-based lubricants. So, finding green carbon dots precursors, optimizing polyelectrolytes types, and tribological tests under other friction pair conditions to evaluate the practical application performance of carbon dots-based nanofluids on the surface of different materials are the possible directions to be explored in the future.
The authors declare that this manuscript is an original work, and the manuscript or part of it has not been published or submitted elsewhere.

Shanqin Ge: Supervision, Formal analysis. Wei Li: Supervision, Investigation. Qingzhao Cai: Supervision, Formal analysis. Jinhong Yu: Supervision. Huan Wang: Supervision. Junhao Wu: Supervision. Genxiang Gong: Supervision. Long Chen: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Tao Cai: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Nan Jiang: Supervision, Resources. Kazuhito Nishimura: Supervision, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We sincerely appreciate the financial supports from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (52075527, 21404114, 51573201 and U1709205), National Key R&D Program of China (2017YFB0406000 and 2017YFE0128600).

ce:section.ce:para: The authors declare no conflict of interest.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.triboint.2024.110285.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.
